original: “You look rather ragged; why don’t you sit down, I’ll buy you a drink,” you tell her, gesturing toward the empty table you’re sitting at. She thinks on it for a moment before rounding the table, pushing one of the seats out of the way, and sitting down on her haunches. “Thanks, I hadn’t realized how tired I am... I need to find this bastard,” she grunts. The waitress comes by, asking her for her order. “Whiskey, on the rocks.” “Are you with the UGC?” you ask, wondering why she approached you randomly. “Not quite, I’m a bounty hunter. You’re a rusher, so I was hoping you had run into him on the frontier. Although in hindsight, I’m glad you haven’t run into him, considering he’s a slaver.” “Wait, how did you know I’m a rusher?” you ask. “Mmm [pc.mf |chico |chica ], you learn how to read people in this line of work. You carry yourself different from a civie or an officer,” she says with a soft chuckle. The waitress returns with your drinks, and she takes a sip of her whiskey. “I don’t think I ever introduced myself. Carmelita Montoya,” she says, reaching across the table to shake. You grip her hand and shake it firmly. “[pc.name] Steele,” you introduce yourself in turn. “Steele... that sounds familiar. That’s the mining company, right?” You nod in affirmation. “How come you’re out here, shouldn’t you be sitting in some boardroom making billions?” You pick up on a slight tilt in her voice, close to what sounds like distaste. “My old man wanted me to go out and earn it instead of just inheriting it. And I wouldn’t want to get it for free, either.” “Really? Well I’ll be — props to you and your father. More people need to understand hard work,” she says, the earlier tilt in her voice gone now, replaced with genuine respect. You continue chatting with her for some time, telling her about your encounters with natives you’ve come across and people you’ve met, and in turn she tells you about some less serious criminals she’s brought to justice. “-and if you believe it, he wasn’t even armed. I mean, he was hiding a pipe in his pocket, pretending it was a gun. Unlucky for him, I had a powerful shield and one hell of a quick draw. The shock pistol doesn’t kill unless it’s overcharged, but it’ll put you on your ass in one shot,” she tells you, chuckling with a rich, feminine laugh. The ice in her second glass clinks as she sets it down. “Well, I should be going; work calls.” She reaches into a pocket inside her jacket and places a chit down on the table. “I know you said you’ll buy me a drink, but consider this my treat,” she says, smiling softly. “Feel free to drop by my ship if you ever feel like it. It’s a jet black stealth ship, you won’t miss it.” Having invited you to her ship, she turns and walks away, and as she does, you can’t help but notice the bulging, latex-covered balls between her hind legs.

original: “Why don’t you tell me about yourself?” you prompt. “Well, I’m Carmelita Montoya, I’m from Terra,” she says, laughing a little at the cookie cutter response to your equally standard question. “But more seriously: yes, I was born on Terra and I’m a full human under all this beauty, if you were wondering. Both of my parents were fine, but well, our relationship has soured.” “I’m not sure if I should ask,” you say, not wanting to pry too much. “It’s fine. My dad is a politician, and he’s a corrupt piece of shit, and my mom didn’t like me describing him like that, so we don’t talk anymore,” she says with a shrug. “You don’t sound too broken up about it.” “It was over twenty years ago — had to move on.” She swirls the drink in her glass before taking a sip. “But despite how I feel about my parents now, back then I had a good childhood. I couldn’t have asked for more loving parents, but as I grew older I started to... well, I hated how I looked. At first, I didn’t really know why, but I bought some mods to change my hair color, and afterward I felt better. Not exactly happy, but better, and I realized that I hated the lack of control. I started using more mods, but they were quite vanilla at first, nothing as major as this,” she says gesturing at her tauric form. “What was your father doing exactly?” “Taking money from corpos while making sure the bills that everyday people wanted to get passed didn’t, and that said corpos could keep doing all kind of scummy stuff.” “Well, I can see why you’d describe him as a corrupt piece of shit,” you say, chuckling softly. “What made you want to join with the UGC?” “Back when I still thought that my dad was helping people, I wanted to do the same, so I joined the peacekeepers. I got to do what he did, but more directly.” “How come you’re not working for UGC anymore?” you ask, recalling her saying she was a former UGC peacekeeper. “I quit. It wasn’t all bad; I got to save lives and put scumbags behind bars, and well, I met my husband while working for UGC.” “Wait, you’re married?!” “... used to be,” she answers quietly, her hand subconsciously fidgeting with her collar tag. Realizing it’s a sore topic, you decide to move on to talk about something else. “So, how did you come into bounty hunting?” you ask after the awkward silence. “I saw some bounty posters after I quit, and realized that if the peacekeepers weren’t going to catch those scumbags, someone would have to, so might as well be me. And since I get paid, I can keep doing it without having to worry about side income. My training from being a peacekeeper helps a lot.” She takes another sip of whiskey before looking at you. “Did you have anything else you wanted to ask?”
context: <> textify(['\\n\\t“Why don’t you tell me about yourself?”'...])

original: “How was your work as a peacekeeper?” “It was great, I wouldn’t trade it for the world. Even if in the end I grew jaded towards the organization, it doesn’t discredit the good I and all the other officers did.” “What did you do while a peacekeeper?” “I started out as a normal beat cop. So most of the time I was making sure people didn’t do anything too stupid, but there was the occasional arrest of dangerous people. After many years of it, I was recommended for special weapons and tactics training.” “Did you accept it?” “Of course. From day one, my goal has been to protect everyday citizens and being in the special unit would help me do just that. Sure, we didn’t always go rescue hostages or bust down dangerous criminals. Sometimes it was riot control, but most of the time we were just training.” “How long have you been a taur? Did you join the force as one?” “No, I was still mostly human when I joined, but with the credits I was getting I could afford more mods, so I continued experimenting until I finally arrived at a form that I find beautiful,” she says, gesturing at herself. “At first, it took some getting used to, but once I learned how to work with it, it became a blessing. I’m faster on foot, and less likely to be knocked down. I was top in the unit until I got injured and couldn’t carry all the gear required. Got a bad back hip,” she says, thumbing over her shoulder. “I can still carry quite a bit, but I would have been risking making it worse if I’d continued, so they shuffled me around until I ended up in an investigation unit.” “You’ve been changing around quite a bit, huh. So how was being a detective?” “Well... it was the best thing ever happened to me, but also the worst. If I hadn’t been made an inspector, I wouldn’t have met my husband... but also I’d still be working as a peacekeeper.” “What happened?” you ask cautiously, the topic being quite sore, obviously. “Eventually, I started looking into corruption within the UGC. At first, I was getting some pushback from the higher up peacekeepers, telling me that I was wasting my time and that I should get back to investigating actual criminals.” She takes a deep breath before carrying on, her voice more shaky and hand gripping the glass tightly. “Well, to me, that was nothing more than them telling me to keep digging deeper. After that didn’t work, they started trying to intimidate me directly. My apartment was repeatedly broken into, the whole place ransacked. I knew I was getting closer... but then...” She hiccups, tears starting to roll down her face. “They killed him. They murdered my husband. He paid the price for my stupidity,” she sobs openly. You reach across the table and place a hand on her shoulder, trying to comfort her. She wipes her eyes and takes another deep breath. “There was an investigation, since he was a peacekeeper too, but they told me that I couldn’t be a part of it as it would be a conflict of interest. The investigation didn’t last long, and soon after it started, the whole thing was swept under the rug. I handed in my badge. I couldn’t keep working for UGC anymore after that. I didn’t tell my buddies in the force what had really happened — they just thought I was heartbroken, and while that was true it was only half of the story. But they helped me stay sober. A lot of the guys in the force crawled into a bottle, and I might’ve too after what happened.” She sighs, swirling the drink around in her glass. “But that’s enough of that. Don’t have much else to tell, and I don’t want to keep talking about it either.”
context: <> textify(['\\n\\t“How was your work as a peacekeeper?”\\n'...])

original: “If you don’t want to tell me then feel free to say so, but I’d like to know a bit about your husband, and how you two came to know each other?” She lets out a soft laugh and takes a swig of whiskey. “Believe it or not, he was a thief. I met him on my first year as a peacekeeper. The first time I met him, I hated the cocky bastard. He was constantly flirting with me, but as I continued pursuing him he started to get to me. His flirting wasn’t just trying to annoy — he really did like me, and I started to like him as well. But he was still a criminal and I was intent on bringing him to justice.” She takes the badge off of her collar and opens it, handing it to you. Inside the locket, she’s got a picture of her and what you assume is her ex-husband. They’re hugging and laughing, wide smiles on both of their faces. “I was always one step behind him, cleaning up after him as he took down some scumbags. He single-handedly ended up taking down the criminal organization that had stabbed him in the back and stole his life’s work. After arresting the head honcho, I finally cornered him but... for some reason, I promised him a head start. At the last second, before his time was up, he swept forward and kissed me, leaving me stunned long enough for him to escape. It was the most shameful moment in my career, but thankfully no one witnessed it.” She blushes, looking embarrassed about admitting her failure, despite it having been years ago. “After that, he went on the down-low for a few months, but resurfaced when some possessions of the gang’s former mastermind were recovered and stored in a museum. They were stolen, of course. At first we thought that he had been behind the theft, but it turned out that a new group of career criminals had stolen them. They were trying to use the gear and insignias to legitimize themselves. Obviously he wasn’t pleased with that and wanted to stop them. So once again, I figured I’d be on the hunt.” “The brass, however, decided that it wasn’t worth the time and resources and they closed the case. I was told to come back and stop chasing my husband-to-be. I refused, because my gut was telling me that it was going to be trouble if I quit then. Eventually I ended up working too closely with him and his crew. The higher ups didn’t like that and I was even arrested. For some reason, he broke me out, so I was on the run with him for a while. During that time I was truly conflicted. My feelings had been growing for years, but he still was a criminal. Though, he wasn’t hurting innocents; he had once explained to me that stealing from your average citizen wasn’t a challenge.” She sips her whiskey again. Exhaling in a long sigh, she swirls the amber liquid in her glass. “But I still kept at it, and somehow I ended up working with him again to arrest the leader of the new gang, making sure that remnants of his past wouldn’t be used as trophies of some greenhorn.” She grins a little, shaking her head softly. “It was the first time I managed to arrest him. His friends had gotten injured in the process, and he made a deal with me: let them go, and he’d come along quietly. I agreed to it, but during the transport we started talking, getting to know each other better, and comparing our experiences of the many heists I had chased him on. In the end, I couldn’t bring myself to throw him behind bars. I told him to book it and lied that he managed to escape.” “Much like last time, he went quiet for a while before popping up again. He had gotten back together with his friends and he was intent on claiming his family fortune. Long story short: someone had acquired the rights to the area where the vault was built. Things got out of hand, and I had to rescue him. Of course, I wasn’t going to just let him go. I wasn’t sure if I was doing it because it was my job or because I cared for him too much to risk losing him. So I had followed — I was going to see the thing to the end. He ended up paying me back for saving his life by saving mine in short order. We had to flee before the whole place came crashing down, and having brushed that closely with death, we both decided to put our differences aside and to try to make it work. He was willing to give himself up and pay his debts to society. But, to both our surprise, he was found innocent. No idea how, but honestly I didn’t care: he was a changed man and he joined the peacekeepers with me.” You can’t help but to laugh a little. “A past crook turned peacekeeper. A perfect combination.” “You’d think that, but he actually did. He was more familiar with how the criminal world works than most of the senior officers. Shortly after that, I was promoted to the special weapons and tactics team, and the rest is history.” She takes the pendant back from you and attaches it back to her collar. “I miss him, but as the old saying goes: It’s better to have loved and lost than never to have loved at all.”
